text:
My quick search hasn't revealed anything so I just wanted to share this here again which a friend mentioned to me today:
<https://www.thewayofcode.com/>

attachments.fallback: The Way of Code: Rick Rubin | The Way of Code: The Timeless Art of Vibe Coding
attachments.text: Rick Rubin brings ancient wisdom to the modern age in The Way of Code, a meditation on the art and science of vibe coding. With Claude by Anthropic, the Grammy-award winning producer and author of The Creative Act turns philosophy into practice with artifacts that can be creatively modified with AI.
attachments.title: Rick Rubin | The Way of Code: The Timeless Art of Vibe Coding

elements.text: My quick search hasn't revealed anything so I just wanted to share this here again which a friend mentioned to me today:

text: A while ago I remember I came across a blog post or spreadsheet contrasting a large number of known non-textual programming languages. One attribute of each project was the modality: node-wire, scratch-like puzzle piece, etc. Does anyone know what I'm talking about or have a link? It is not The Whole Code Catalog or any of the linked surveys, nor is it the "Future of Coding or Programming: Project Comparison" spreadsheet.

elements.text: A while ago I remember I came across a blog post or spreadsheet contrasting a large number of known non-textual programming languages. One attribute of each project was the modality: node-wire, scratch-like puzzle piece, etc. Does anyone know what I'm talking about or have a link? It is not The Whole Code Catalog or any of the linked surveys
, nor is it the "Future of Coding or Programming: Project Comparison" spreadsheet.

text:
Here's the link to the spreadsheet in case others are wondering:

<https://docs.google.com/spreadsheets/d/12sTu7RT-s_QlAupY1v-3DfI1Mm9NEX5YMWWTDAKHLfc/edit?gid=0#gid=0>

elements.text: Here's the link to the spreadsheet in case others are wondering:

text: Nope. I could honestly be misremembering the contents and just be thinking of the project comparison spreadsheet, but I swear the spreadsheet that I found had way more projects, and definitely had this modality column
